title: Pediatric blood culture collection
executiveSummary: Obtain blood cultures before antibiotic administration whenever possible, adjusting the volume to the child’s weight, using a strict aseptic technique, and without delaying antibiotics in severe sepsis.
body:
## Steps to perform

1. Confirm the clinical indication; identify the patient (two identifiers), weigh the child, and document the time, anatomical site, and reason for the culture.
2. Explain the procedure to the family; arrange therapeutic restraint and analgesia (24% sucrose in infants, topical lidocaine if time permits).
3. Prepare pediatric blood culture bottles (aerobic and anaerobic if indicated); disinfect the bottle tops with 70% alcohol and allow to dry.
4. Perform hand hygiene (WHO Moment 2).
5. Disinfect the skin with an antiseptic using circular friction; expand the prepped area; allow to dry for ≥30 seconds; do not re-touch the disinfected zone.
6. Perform peripheral venipuncture using an aseptic technique; extract the appropriate volume according to weight (see table in Scientific rationale).
7. Inoculate the bottles in order according to the system used: vacuum system (aerobic first); syringe system (anaerobic first if applicable).
8. Mix by gentle inversion; label on a clean surface with the patient’s name, date, time, site, and weight.
9. Perform hand hygiene upon completion; transport to the laboratory immediately.
10. Document the number of sets, volume per bottle, prior antibiotics, and the start time of empiric antibiotics.
11. Communicate any positive pathogen result, suspected contamination, or clinical deterioration.

## Scientific rationale

### Indications

- Suspected sepsis or septic shock.
- Fever in a high-risk infant (AAP strategy).
- Fever without an apparent source with clinical instability or immunocompromise.
- Suspected endocarditis or catheter-related bloodstream infection.

### Evidence and recommendations

> **Pediatric SSC (2026):** Obtain cultures prior to administering empiric antibiotics if it does not delay their initiation. In septic shock: **do not delay antibiotics** due to difficult venipuncture; draw cultures in parallel (Weiss et al., 2026).

### Timing and number of sets

- Ideal timing: prior to antibiotics; it is not mandatory to coincide with a febrile peak.
- Severe sepsis: minimum of 2 sets from different anatomical sites, drawn simultaneously.
- Endocarditis: 3 sets drawn from different sites.

### Volume according to weight

| Child’s weight | Volume per bottle |
| --- | --- |
| < 1 kg | 0.5–1 ml |
| 1–5 kg | 1–3 ml |
| 5–10 kg | 3–5 ml |
| 10–20 kg | 5–10 ml |
| > 20 kg | 10–15 ml |

Do not overfill pediatric bottles; respect the manufacturer’s maximum limits.

### Puncture site

- Prefer peripheral venipuncture at distinct sites.
- Central catheter blood: only when following the differential time to positivity/back-culture protocol (`ret-p001`).
- Do not routinely discard blood prior to peripheral blood culture collection.

### Prevention of contamination

- Do not touch the bottle top after disinfection.
- In infants: use a strict aseptic technique due to the higher clinical impact of false positives (SATI, 2007; CGEE, 2024).

### Escalation

- If it is impossible to obtain a sample in severe sepsis: escalate vascular access (intraosseous, central) without delaying antibiotic therapy.

bibliography.citation: Weiss SL, et al. Surviving Sepsis Campaign International Guidelines for the Management of Septic Shock and Sepsis-Associated Organ Dysfunction in Children (2026).
American Academy of Pediatrics. Clinical Practice Guideline: Febrile Infants 2021.
SATI — Toma de muestras microbiológicas en UTI (Comité de Infectología Crítica).
Guía de práctica clínica enfermera sobre hemocultivos — volúmenes pediatrics (Consejo General de Enfermería de España).
Cornistein W, et al. Central venous catheter-associated infections. SATI/SADI intersociety update 2025.
SATI — Comité de Infectología Crítica. Guías y consensos.
